text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic adrenalectomy to remove a hemorrhagic pheochromocytoma, utilizing minimally invasive techniques to access and excise the affected adrenal gland.
Authors have nothing to disclose.
There are sixty-five reported cases of hemorrhagic pheochromocytoma.
Emergent surgical mortality has been reported to be as high as twenty-nine to thirty-four percent.
Survival has significantly increased with pre-medication and elective surgery.
Our patient is a forty-six year old female with no prior medical history who presented to an outside hospital with a day of nausea and acute right-sided back pain.
She reported three years of intermittent headache, palpitation, and a hundred pound weight loss over the past year.
She endorsed a blood pressure of two hundred six over one forty-five and a heart rate of one forty-one as well as a diffuse abdominal tenderness.
She was in diabetic ketoacidosis with a rapid decrease of her hemoglobin and suffered from acute liver and kidney failure.
She was intubated, treated with nicartapine, and packed red blood cell.
She was then transferred to the Mount Sinai Hospital.
At Mount Sinai, a CT angiogram was emergently performed.
It showed a large, nine-centimeter hemorrhagic adrenal mass in the right retroperitoneum, as shown in the image.
Her adrenal function laboratory analysis was consistent with pheochromocytoma.
After stabilization, she was discharged on phenoxybenzamine, insulin, and propranolol.
Elective adrenalectomy was performed two months after her discharge.
The patient is positioned in a left lateral decubitus position on the operating table, preparing for the adrenalectomy procedure.
Four laparoscopic ports are visibly placed along the left costal margin, providing access for the adrenalectomy procedure.
A laparoscopic liver retractor is inserted to elevate the right lobe of the liver, revealing a large adrenal mass beneath.
The surgeon incises the peritoneal reflection up to the diaphragm, including the triangular ligament, using laparoscopic instruments.
The surgeon dissects the space between the adrenal gland and the inferior vena cava, revealing the right adrenal vein.
The surgeon carefully isolates the right adrenal vein, which is exposed between the adrenal gland and the inferior vena cava.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealing device to carefully divide the isolated right adrenal vein, ensuring precise separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon continues the medial dissection upwards, carefully navigating around small vessels emerging from the inferior vena cava, ensuring these structures are preserved during the procedure.
The surgeon encounters a second adrenal vein during the dissection, highlighting its rare occurrence in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully isolates and divides the second adrenal vein using a vessel sealing device, following its exposure between the adrenal gland and the inferior vena cava.
The surgeon carefully mobilizes the inferior and lateral attachments of the adrenal gland, ensuring clear separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon isolates the adrenal gland, ensuring a healthy rim of periadrenal fatty tissue surrounds it, preparing for further dissection.

The surgeon encounters difficulty while dissecting the posterior adrenal gland due to the patient's history of acute adrenal hemorrhage, requiring careful manipulation to separate the gland from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses a suction device in the left hand to control bleeding during the adrenalectomy procedure.
The surgeon encounters bleeding in the posterior space while dissecting the adrenal gland, taking care to ensure no tumor tissue is left in the retroperitoneum.
The surgeon alternates dissection between the lateral and superior attachments of the adrenal gland, using a suction device to lift the gland while achieving hemostasis with the opposite hand.
The surgeon places the adrenal tumor into a retrieval sac and extracts it from the abdominal cavity, completing the removal process.
Tumor margins were negative.
